influenza.name: Influenza (Flu)
influenza.schedule: Annually, ideally October–November before flu season
influenza.description: Seasonal influenza vaccine recommended annually for all adults. Protects against 3–4 influenza strains selected by the WHO each year based on global surveillance data.
sources: WHO Position Paper on Influenza Vaccines 2023

covid19.name: COVID-19
covid19.schedule: Primary series + updated booster annually (similar to flu shot)
covid19.description: mRNA or protein subunit vaccine targeting SARS-CoV-2. Updated formulations are released annually to match circulating variants. Highly effective at preventing severe illness and hospitalization.
sources: WHO COVID-19 Vaccines Technical Advisory Group 2024

tdap.name: Tdap / Td
tdap.schedule: 1 dose Tdap (if never received), then Td booster every 10 years
tdap.description: Combination vaccine protecting against tetanus (lockjaw), diphtheria, and pertussis (whooping cough). Pertussis is highly contagious and can be fatal in infants. Tetanus spores persist in soil worldwide.
sources: WHO Position Paper on Tetanus Vaccines 2017

mmr.name: MMR (Measles, Mumps, Rubella)
mmr.schedule: 1–2 doses if not previously immune; do NOT give during pregnancy
mmr.description: Live attenuated vaccine providing lifelong immunity against three highly contagious viral diseases. Measles can cause severe complications including encephalitis. Rubella during pregnancy causes congenital rubella syndrome.

varicella.name: Varicella (Chickenpox)
varicella.schedule: 2 doses (4–8 weeks apart) if no prior chickenpox or vaccination
varicella.description: Live attenuated vaccine for adults with no prior chickenpox infection or vaccination. Varicella is more severe in adults than children. After infection, the virus lies dormant and can reactivate as shingles.

zoster.name: Herpes Zoster (Recombinant)
zoster.schedule: 2 doses, 2–6 months apart; non-live vaccine — safe even for immunocompromised patients
zoster.description: Recombinant adjuvanted vaccine against herpes zoster (shingles) and postherpetic neuralgia. 91% efficacy. Per PAPDI 2025, recommended at any adult age for patients with diabetes, immunocompromised conditions, HIV, heart disease, lung disease, kidney disease, liver disease, or cancer — not only adults 50+.
sources: WHO Position Paper on Herpes Zoster Vaccines 2014

hpv.name: HPV (Human Papillomavirus)
hpv.schedule: 2 doses (0 and 6–12 months) if starting before 15; 3 doses if starting 15–45
hpv.description: Protects against the HPV strains responsible for cervical, oropharyngeal, anal, vulvar, vaginal, and penile cancers, as well as genital warts. Over 90% of HPV-related cancers are preventable with vaccination.

rsv.name: RSV (Respiratory Syncytial Virus)
rsv.schedule: 1 dose; recommended annually for high-risk adults
rsv.description: Recombinant protein subunit vaccine against Respiratory Syncytial Virus, a leading cause of lower respiratory tract illness in older adults and immunocompromised patients. RSV causes ~100,000 hospitalizations per year in adults ≥65 globally. Per PAPDI 2025, listed as a priority vaccine alongside Herpes Zoster, Influenza, and Pneumococcal for adults with chronic conditions.

pneumococcal.name: Pneumococcal
pneumococcal.schedule: 1 dose (higher-valency conjugate vaccine preferred); or sequential schedule per clinical guideline
pneumococcal.description: Prevents invasive pneumococcal disease caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae, including pneumonia, meningitis, and sepsis. Streptococcus pneumoniae is the leading cause of bacterial pneumonia in adults worldwide.

hepatitis_a.name: Hepatitis A
hepatitis_a.schedule: 2 doses (0 and 6–12 months); single-antigen or combination with Hep B
hepatitis_a.description: Prevents hepatitis A, transmitted via contaminated food and water. Infection risk is elevated in travelers to developing countries. No chronic carrier state, but acute illness can be severe, particularly in adults.

hepatitis_b.name: Hepatitis B
hepatitis_b.schedule: 2-dose series (1 month apart) or standard 3-dose series; higher dose for dialysis patients
hepatitis_b.description: Prevents hepatitis B, a bloodborne virus that can cause chronic liver disease, cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma. 296 million people live with chronic HBV infection worldwide. Indonesia has high HBV endemicity.
sources: Kemenkes RI — Indonesia HBV Control Program 2022

meningococcal.name: Meningococcal (MenACWY + MenB)
meningococcal.schedule: MenACWY: 1–2 doses; MenB: 2–3 doses depending on brand
meningococcal.description: Prevents bacterial meningitis caused by Neisseria meningitidis. Meningococcal disease kills within 24–48 hours and causes permanent disability (hearing loss, limb amputation) in 20% of survivors.

typhoid.name: Typhoid
typhoid.schedule: Injectable Vi polysaccharide: 1 dose (valid 2 years); Oral Ty21a: 4 capsules (valid 5 years)
typhoid.description: Prevents typhoid fever (Salmonella typhi), transmitted via contaminated food/water. Endemic in South/Southeast Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, and Latin America. Indonesia reports >600 cases per 100,000 annually.

yellow_fever.name: Yellow Fever
yellow_fever.schedule: 1 dose (considered lifelong after 2016 WHO revision); legally required for some countries
yellow_fever.description: Mosquito-borne viral hemorrhagic fever. Required for entry to 43 countries in Africa and South America. Single dose provides lifelong immunity. Must be administered at certified Yellow Fever Vaccination Centers.

japanese_encephalitis.name: Japanese Encephalitis
japanese_encephalitis.schedule: 2 doses (days 0 and 28); booster at 1 year if ongoing risk
japanese_encephalitis.description: Mosquito-borne viral encephalitis. 68,000 cases/year in Asia; 30% mortality, 50% of survivors have permanent neurologic disability. Risk is highest in rural rice-farming areas of Asia during transmission season.
